questions.question: What is the minimum token threshold for prompt caching to activate on Sonnet and Opus?
options: 2,048 tokens for Sonnet and Opus; 1,024 tokens for Haiku
1,000 tokens for all models
4,096 tokens for Sonnet and Opus; 2,048 tokens for Haiku
Any length — the API automatically caches all repeated prefixes
questions.explanation: Caching only activates above the minimum threshold: 2,048 tokens (Sonnet/Opus) or 1,024 tokens (Haiku). Shorter prefixes are processed normally every request.

questions.question: You have a 5,000-token system prompt that is identical across all users. Where should you place the `cache_control` breakpoint?
options: At the end of the system prompt — everything before the breakpoint is cached; dynamic per-user content goes after
At the beginning of the system prompt — Claude caches from the very first token
On each user message individually, since user messages are what repeat most often
In the API request headers, not in the message content
questions.explanation: The breakpoint marks the end of the stable prefix. Everything before it is cached and re-used. Dynamic content (user messages) must come after the breakpoint to avoid cache misses.

questions.question: A cache breakpoint is set correctly but subsequent requests are not hitting the cache. What is the most likely cause?
options: The prefix is not exactly identical — even one character difference (timestamp, username, trailing space) invalidates the cache
The cache TTL expired — increase the cache lifetime in API settings
A recent model version update invalidated all existing cache entries
The cache_control breakpoint must be re-declared on every request to remain active
questions.explanation: Prompt caching uses exact-match on the prefix. Any variation — a timestamp, a user name, a different whitespace character — causes a cache miss and a full cache write.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of `cache_control` breakpoints allowed in a single request?
options: Unlimited — any content block can have a cache_control marker
questions.explanation: A maximum of 4 cache_control breakpoints are allowed per request. Place them strategically after the longest stable prefixes.

questions.question: How long does a prompt cache entry persist after its last use?
options: 5 minutes — each cache hit resets the TTL clock
24 hours after the first write
Until the API session or authentication token expires
Permanently, until you manually invalidate it
questions.explanation: Cache entries have a 5-minute ephemeral TTL. A hit resets the clock. If no request hits the entry within 5 minutes, the next request is a cache write (paying full input token cost).

questions.question: What is the cost discount for the Batch API compared to the real-time Messages API?
options: 50% discount on both input and output tokens; results returned within 24 hours
25% discount on input tokens only; output tokens are billed at the standard rate
75% discount on output tokens; no discount on input tokens
30% flat discount across all token types
questions.explanation: The Batch API offers a 50% discount on both input and output tokens in exchange for asynchronous processing. Results are returned within 24 hours.

questions.question: You submit a Batch API job. How do you retrieve the results?
options: Poll `batches.retrieve(batch_id)` until `processing_status` is `"ended"`, then iterate `batches.results(batch_id)` — each result has a `custom_id` and a `result` object
Results are automatically sent to a webhook URL you specify at job submission
Call `batches.get_results(batch_id)` immediately after submission — it blocks until results are ready
Subscribe to a `batch_complete` event via the streaming API
questions.explanation: Poll for status "ended", then call .results() to iterate. Each result object includes your custom_id for correlation and a result with the full message or error.

questions.question: Which metric specifically measures how long a user waits before seeing the first word of a streamed response?
options: Time-to-first-token (TTFT) — streaming dramatically reduces this even when total generation time is the same
Total latency — the time from request to full response delivery
Perceived response time (PRT) — a composite metric for user experience
Token generation rate (TGR) — tokens produced per second after the first token
questions.explanation: TTFT measures perceived responsiveness. With streaming, users see the first word in milliseconds. Total latency measures the full response time and is a separate metric.

questions.question: You want to route simple classification tasks to Haiku and complex reasoning to Sonnet. What is this pattern called?
options: Model routing (or model tiering) — a lightweight classifier selects the cheapest model that meets quality requirements
Prompt compression — reduce prompt length so simpler models can handle it
Cascading inference — try Haiku first, escalate to Sonnet if confidence is low
Adaptive sampling — adjust sampling parameters based on task complexity
questions.explanation: Model routing matches task complexity to model capability. Simple tasks use cheap, fast Haiku; complex ones use Sonnet. Done well, it cuts cost significantly without sacrificing quality.

questions.question: You run the same eval suite on Prompt V1 and V2. One test case drops from 4.2 to 2.8 on the clarity criterion. What does this indicate?
options: A regression — Prompt V2 is significantly worse on clarity for that case; investigate before deploying
Normal variance — score differences under 2 points are within acceptable LLM judge margins
Prompt V2 is optimizing a different dimension; the clarity tradeoff may be acceptable
The eval is unreliable — LLM judges produce noisy scores and this delta is not significant
questions.explanation: A drop from 4.2 to 2.8 is a meaningful regression. Before shipping V2, understand what change caused it and decide whether the tradeoff is acceptable — don't ignore it.

questions.question: Why should you use a stronger model (Sonnet or Opus) as your LLM judge even if you use Haiku for production?
options: The judge's quality determines eval reliability — a weak judge gives noisy, miscalibrated scores that make your eval useless
Stronger models run in a separate billing environment so judge calls don't affect production costs
Anthropic's eval guidelines require a judge model at least one tier above the production model
Judge quality only matters for creative tasks; Haiku is sufficient for factual or structured evals
questions.explanation: Evals run on a sample, not every request, so paying for a strong judge is cheap relative to the value of reliable quality signal. A weak judge defeats the purpose of evaluating.

questions.question: What is the key rule for separating your prompt optimization set from your evaluation set?
options: Never optimize prompts against the same test cases you use to measure quality — doing so causes overfitting to the eval
The training set must be at least 10× larger than the evaluation set for valid results
Use a different model version for training runs versus evaluation runs
Test cases must be randomly re-sampled fresh on every evaluation run
questions.explanation: Optimizing against your eval set measures performance on those specific examples, not real-world generalization. You'll score well on the eval while shipping a worse product.

questions.question: Output tokens are priced at 3–5× the rate of input tokens. Name two strategies to reduce output token cost.
options: Explicit length/format constraints ("answer in one sentence") and tight `max_tokens` for constrained tasks
Enabling streaming mode and increasing temperature to reach answers faster
Using the Batch API and compressing the system prompt
Switching to an older model version and adding stop_sequences to end responses earlier
questions.explanation: Length constraints and tight max_tokens directly cap output tokens. Using structured output (JSON instead of prose) and routing verbose tasks to Haiku also reduce output spend.

questions.question: You add prompt caching to a multi-turn customer support bot. Where should the cache breakpoint be placed?
options: At the end of the growing conversation history — after the last assistant message, caching the accumulated conversation so far
Only on the system prompt — conversation history should not be cached as it changes every turn
At the beginning of each new user message
After every third exchange to balance cache freshness and cost
questions.explanation: Cache as much of the stable accumulated conversation as possible. Place the breakpoint after the latest assistant message. As new messages arrive, move the breakpoint forward.

questions.question: Name three metrics you should monitor in a production Claude deployment.
options: Any three of: latency (TTFT and total), error rate (4xx/5xx), token usage per request, refusal rate, user satisfaction (CSAT), cache hit rate, cost per request
Model version, API key rotation frequency, and system prompt length
CPU utilization, memory consumption, and disk I/O on your inference servers
Request count, response word count, and session duration
questions.explanation: Core production metrics: latency, error rate, token cost, refusal rate, user satisfaction, cache efficiency. Together they give you visibility into quality, cost, and reliability.
